questions.stem: A 54-year-old man with a history of a myocardial infarction 3 months ago presents to the cardiology clinic for management of recurrent premature ventricular complexes. His echocardiogram shows an ejection fraction of 35%. The cardiologist avoids prescribing flecainide. Which of the following best explains the rationale for avoiding this drug in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Flecainide causes excessive QT prolongation and torsades de pointes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flecainide has strong negative inotropic effects that worsen heart failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Flecainide increases mortality in post-MI patients with structural heart disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Flecainide has significant drug interactions with all beta-blockers

choices.label: E
choices.text: Flecainide is only effective for supraventricular arrhythmias, not ventricular

explanation.summary: The Cardiac Arrhythmia Suppression Trial (CAST) demonstrated that flecainide (a Class IC antiarrhythmic) increased mortality in post-MI patients with structural heart disease, despite effectively suppressing PVCs. This is the primary reason it is contraindicated in patients with ischemic heart disease and reduced ejection fraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The CAST trial showed that flecainide and encainide increased sudden cardiac death and overall mortality in post-MI patients, establishing a clear contraindication for Class IC agents in structural heart disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Flecainide primarily prolongs the QRS duration (sodium channel blockade) rather than the QT interval. Torsades de pointes is more characteristic of Class IA and Class III agents.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While flecainide does have mild negative inotropic effects, the primary concern is proarrhythmia and increased mortality, not worsening heart failure alone.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Flecainide does not have contraindicated interactions with all beta-blockers. In fact, beta-blockers are sometimes co-administered to mitigate flecainide's proarrhythmic risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flecainide is effective for both supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias; the issue is safety in the post-MI setting, not lack of efficacy.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman is started on procainamide for recurrent sustained ventricular tachycardia. After 8 months of therapy, she develops joint pain, pleuritic chest pain, and a malar rash. Laboratory studies reveal anti-histone antibodies. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which procainamide causes this adverse effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct cytotoxic damage to synovial membranes and serosal surfaces

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drug-induced inhibition of DNA methylation leading to autoimmune activation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Type I hypersensitivity reaction mediated by IgE antibodies

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immune complex deposition secondary to chronic sodium channel blockade

choices.label: E
choices.text: Disruption of central immune tolerance through thymic epithelial damage

explanation.summary: Procainamide is well known for causing drug-induced lupus erythematosus (DILE), which presents with arthralgias, serositis, and positive anti-histone antibodies. The mechanism involves inhibition of DNA methylation by the drug's metabolite (N-acetylprocainamide pathway), leading to T-cell autoreactivity and autoimmune manifestations.
explanation.whyCorrect: Procainamide and its metabolites inhibit DNA methyltransferase, causing hypomethylation of T-cell DNA. This leads to overexpression of adhesion molecules and autoreactivity, producing a lupus-like syndrome characterized by anti-histone antibodies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The damage is immune-mediated, not directly cytotoxic. Procainamide does not cause direct tissue destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Drug-induced lupus is not a Type I (IgE-mediated) hypersensitivity reaction. It is an autoimmune phenomenon involving altered T-cell function.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While immune complexes may play a role in lupus nephritis, the fundamental mechanism of drug-induced lupus involves epigenetic changes, not a direct consequence of sodium channel blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Drug-induced lupus involves peripheral immune dysregulation through DNA hypomethylation, not central tolerance breakdown via thymic damage.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome presents with a wide-complex irregular tachycardia at a rate of 220 bpm. His blood pressure is 100/70 mmHg. The emergency physician chooses to administer procainamide intravenously. Which of the following properties of procainamide makes it preferable to AV nodal blocking agents in this scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It slows conduction through the accessory pathway while avoiding enhanced conduction through the bypass tract

choices.label: B
choices.text: It selectively blocks conduction through the AV node without affecting ventricular repolarization

choices.label: C
choices.text: It increases the refractory period of atrial tissue without any effect on ventricular tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: It has pure vagolytic effects that terminate the reentrant circuit at the AV node

choices.label: E
choices.text: It acts exclusively on potassium channels to prolong accessory pathway refractoriness

explanation.summary: In WPW with atrial fibrillation (wide-complex irregular tachycardia), AV nodal blocking agents (adenosine, verapamil, digoxin) are contraindicated because they may enhance conduction through the accessory pathway, potentially leading to ventricular fibrillation. Procainamide slows conduction and increases refractoriness in the accessory pathway, making it a safe and effective choice.
explanation.whyCorrect: Procainamide blocks sodium channels in the accessory pathway, slowing conduction and prolonging its refractory period. Unlike AV nodal blockers, it does not divert all conduction through the bypass tract, thereby reducing the risk of ventricular fibrillation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Procainamide does not selectively block the AV node; it affects both the accessory pathway and ventricular tissue. Its advantage is its effect on the bypass tract, not AV nodal selectivity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Procainamide affects both atrial and ventricular tissue, prolonging action potential duration in both. It does not have atrial-selective effects.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Procainamide does have mild anticholinergic (vagolytic) properties, but this is not the primary reason for its use in WPW. The key benefit is sodium channel blockade in the accessory pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Procainamide is primarily a sodium channel blocker (Class IA), though it also has some potassium channel blocking effects. It does not act exclusively on potassium channels.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with atrial flutter is started on quinidine without prior rate control. Two days later, she presents with syncope and her ECG shows a ventricular rate of 180 bpm with a 1:1 flutter-to-ventricular conduction pattern. Which of the following best explains the paradoxical increase in ventricular rate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Quinidine's sodium channel blockade increased automaticity in the AV node

choices.label: B
choices.text: Quinidine's potassium channel blockade paradoxically shortened the AV nodal refractory period

choices.label: C
choices.text: Quinidine caused a proarrhythmic conversion of atrial flutter to ventricular tachycardia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Quinidine enhanced sympathetic outflow through direct stimulation of cardiac beta-1 receptors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Quinidine slowed the atrial flutter rate, allowing 1:1 AV conduction, and its vagolytic effect enhanced AV nodal conduction

explanation.summary: Quinidine can paradoxically increase the ventricular rate in atrial flutter through two mechanisms: (1) it slows the atrial flutter rate from ~300 bpm to ~200 bpm, making 1:1 AV conduction possible, and (2) its vagolytic (anticholinergic) properties enhance AV nodal conduction. This is why an AV nodal blocking agent should always be given before initiating quinidine for atrial flutter.
explanation.whyCorrect: By reducing the atrial rate and simultaneously enhancing AV conduction through vagolysis, quinidine allows every flutter wave to be conducted to the ventricles, resulting in a dangerously fast ventricular rate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Quinidine does not increase AV nodal automaticity. The mechanism involves reduced atrial rate combined with enhanced AV conduction, not increased nodal automaticity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While quinidine does block potassium channels, the enhanced AV conduction is due to its anticholinergic (vagolytic) properties, not a direct effect of potassium channel blockade on the AV node.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The rhythm remains atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction, not ventricular tachycardia. The wide QRS may mimic VT, but the mechanism is rapid AV conduction of flutter waves.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Quinidine does not directly stimulate beta-1 receptors. Its effect on AV conduction is mediated by anticholinergic activity, not sympathetic stimulation.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after intentional ingestion of a large quantity of flecainide tablets. His ECG shows markedly widened QRS complexes and hypotension. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment to address the cardiac toxicity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous isoproterenol

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transvenous cardiac pacing

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous amiodarone

explanation.summary: Flecainide toxicity causes severe sodium channel blockade, manifested by QRS widening and cardiovascular collapse. Intravenous sodium bicarbonate is the first-line treatment because it increases extracellular sodium concentration (helping overcome sodium channel blockade) and alkalinizes the blood (reducing drug binding to sodium channels).
explanation.whyCorrect: Sodium bicarbonate provides a sodium load that competes with flecainide at sodium channels and creates an alkaline environment that decreases the drug's affinity for the sodium channel, thereby narrowing the QRS and improving conduction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Magnesium sulfate is used for torsades de pointes and hypomagnesemia-related arrhythmias, not for sodium channel blocker toxicity with QRS widening.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Isoproterenol may be used for bradycardia or torsades de pointes but is not first-line for sodium channel blocker toxicity and may worsen hemodynamic instability.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transvenous pacing may be considered if there is refractory bradycardia, but it does not address the underlying sodium channel blockade and is not the initial treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amiodarone would further depress conduction and is contraindicated in the setting of sodium channel blocker toxicity with QRS prolongation.

questions.stem: A pharmacology student is studying the kinetics of sodium channel blockade by different Class I antiarrhythmics. She learns that lidocaine has rapid binding and unbinding kinetics to the sodium channel. Which of the following is the primary clinical implication of this property?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lidocaine is most effective at slow heart rates when channels spend more time in the resting state

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lidocaine prolongs the QT interval more than quinidine at therapeutic concentrations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lidocaine is the preferred agent for rate control in atrial fibrillation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lidocaine produces minimal QRS prolongation at normal heart rates but greater blockade at faster rates

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lidocaine equally affects sodium channels regardless of the heart rate

explanation.summary: Lidocaine (Class IB) has rapid on-off kinetics, meaning it binds and unbinds from sodium channels quickly. At normal heart rates, channels recover fully between beats, so lidocaine produces minimal effect on QRS duration. At faster rates, there is insufficient time for complete unbinding, resulting in accumulation of blockade (use-dependence). This makes lidocaine particularly effective for tachyarrhythmias.
explanation.whyCorrect: The rapid kinetics of lidocaine mean it dissociates from sodium channels during normal diastolic intervals, producing little effect at physiological rates. During tachycardia, shortened diastole prevents full recovery, so blockade accumulates, providing rate-dependent suppression.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lidocaine preferentially binds to activated and inactivated sodium channels during tachycardia, not to resting-state channels at slow heart rates. Slow rates allow full recovery and reduce its effect.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lidocaine shortens or has no significant effect on the QT interval. It shortens repolarization, unlike Class IA agents such as quinidine that prolong it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lidocaine has minimal effect on atrial tissue and is not used for atrial fibrillation rate control. It is primarily effective for ventricular arrhythmias.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The hallmark of lidocaine is rate-dependent (use-dependent) blockade. Its effect increases with faster heart rates, which is a fundamental characteristic of its rapid kinetics.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a structurally normal heart develops paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. His cardiologist prescribes flecainide as a "pill-in-the-pocket" approach and co-prescribes a beta-blocker. Which of the following best explains why a beta-blocker is added to this regimen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To prevent flecainide-induced QT prolongation and torsades de pointes

choices.label: B
choices.text: To counteract the anticholinergic effects of flecainide on the sinoatrial node

choices.label: C
choices.text: To prevent the risk of 1:1 AV conduction if atrial fibrillation organizes into atrial flutter

choices.label: D
choices.text: To enhance flecainide's absorption from the gastrointestinal tract

choices.label: E
choices.text: To prevent reflex tachycardia caused by flecainide's vasodilatory properties

explanation.summary: Flecainide can organize atrial fibrillation into atrial flutter by slowing the atrial rate. If the slowed flutter rate allows 1:1 AV conduction, it can result in a dangerously rapid ventricular rate. A beta-blocker (or other AV nodal blocking agent) is co-prescribed to prevent this by limiting AV conduction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Class IC agents like flecainide can convert atrial fibrillation to atrial flutter with a slower atrial rate, making 1:1 AV conduction possible. The beta-blocker ensures AV nodal blockade prevents this dangerous scenario.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Flecainide does not significantly prolong the QT interval. QT prolongation and torsades are associated with Class IA and Class III agents, not Class IC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Flecainide has minimal anticholinergic activity. This concern is more relevant to quinidine and disopyramide, which have prominent vagolytic effects.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-blockers do not enhance the gastrointestinal absorption of flecainide. There is no pharmacokinetic basis for this interaction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flecainide is not a vasodilator and does not cause reflex tachycardia. The concern is proarrhythmic organization of AF into flutter, not hemodynamic reflex responses.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and recurrent ventricular tachycardia is being evaluated for antiarrhythmic therapy. Her physician avoids disopyramide specifically for rate control purposes but considers it for another indication. Which of the following is a unique property of disopyramide that makes it potentially useful in this patient's underlying condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has potent vasodilatory effects that reduce afterload

choices.label: B
choices.text: It selectively blocks late sodium current without affecting peak sodium current

choices.label: C
choices.text: It has intrinsic sympathomimetic activity that maintains cardiac output

choices.label: D
choices.text: It blocks calcium channels in the interventricular septum to reduce hypertrophy

choices.label: E
choices.text: It has strong negative inotropic effects that can reduce left ventricular outflow tract obstruction

explanation.summary: Disopyramide has the strongest negative inotropic effect among Class IA agents. In hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) with left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) obstruction, this property is therapeutically beneficial because it reduces the dynamic obstruction by decreasing the force of ventricular contraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Disopyramide's pronounced negative inotropy reduces systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve and LVOT gradient in HCM, making it a unique pharmacologic option for symptomatic obstructive HCM.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Disopyramide does not have vasodilatory effects. In fact, vasodilation would be harmful in HCM as it would worsen the LVOT gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Disopyramide blocks peak sodium current, not selectively late sodium current. Ranolazine is the agent known for selective late sodium current blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Disopyramide does not have intrinsic sympathomimetic activity. Some beta-blockers (e.g., pindolol) have this property, but it is not characteristic of Class IA agents.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Disopyramide is a sodium channel blocker, not a calcium channel blocker. It does not directly reduce myocardial hypertrophy.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a wide-complex tachycardia. An ECG from his prior visit shows a right bundle branch block at baseline. After intravenous lidocaine administration, the tachycardia terminates and his rhythm returns to sinus with the pre-existing RBBB. Which of the following best explains why lidocaine was effective for his ventricular arrhythmia but did not worsen his bundle branch block?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lidocaine only blocks sodium channels in ventricular myocytes, not in the His-Purkinje system

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lidocaine has a longer binding time constant that protects conduction tissue from excessive blockade

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lidocaine primarily acts by blocking potassium channels, which does not affect conduction velocity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lidocaine preferentially binds to sodium channels in ischemic and depolarized tissue rather than normally polarized conduction tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lidocaine has strong calcium channel blocking properties that stabilize the His-Purkinje system

explanation.summary: Lidocaine preferentially binds to sodium channels in the inactivated state, which predominates in ischemic, depolarized, or rapidly firing tissue. Normal conduction tissue (even with RBBB) that is not ischemic maintains normal resting membrane potential, so lidocaine has minimal effect on it. This selectivity allows lidocaine to suppress ventricular arrhythmias arising from abnormal tissue without significantly worsening conduction in relatively healthy tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lidocaine's state-dependent binding means it preferentially affects inactivated sodium channels in depolarized or ischemic myocardium. The RBBB tissue, while structurally abnormal, is not depolarized in the same way, so lidocaine spares it.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lidocaine does act on the His-Purkinje system; it is not selectively excluded from conduction tissue. Its selectivity is based on channel state, not tissue type.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lidocaine has rapid (short) binding kinetics, not a longer binding time constant. This rapid unbinding is what allows normal tissue to recover between heartbeats.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lidocaine is primarily a sodium channel blocker (Class IB). It shortens repolarization slightly but does not primarily act through potassium channel blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lidocaine has negligible calcium channel blocking activity. Its antiarrhythmic effect is almost entirely mediated through sodium channel blockade.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman is started on quinidine for recurrent atrial fibrillation. Three days later, she develops nausea, diarrhea, tinnitus, and blurred vision. Which of the following terms best describes this constellation of adverse effects?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Quinidine syncope

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drug-induced lupus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anticholinergic toxidrome

choices.label: D
choices.text: Serotonin syndrome

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cinchonism

explanation.summary: Cinchonism is a syndrome of adverse effects associated with quinidine (and quinine), including tinnitus, hearing loss, blurred vision, headache, nausea, and diarrhea. The term derives from the cinchona tree bark from which these drugs are derived.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of tinnitus, visual disturbances, and GI symptoms (nausea, diarrhea) is the classic presentation of cinchonism, a dose-related toxicity of quinidine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Quinidine syncope refers to loss of consciousness from quinidine-induced torsades de pointes (due to QT prolongation), not the constellation of GI and sensory symptoms described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Drug-induced lupus presents with arthralgias, serositis, and anti-histone antibodies. It is classically associated with procainamide, not quinidine, and does not cause tinnitus.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anticholinergic toxidrome presents with dry mouth, urinary retention, mydriasis, tachycardia, and confusion. Diarrhea and tinnitus are not anticholinergic features.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Serotonin syndrome presents with hyperthermia, clonus, agitation, and diaphoresis, typically caused by serotonergic drugs. Quinidine is not a serotonergic agent.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man undergoes electrophysiology testing. During the study, the physician infuses a sodium channel blocker and observes that the effective refractory period of the ventricular myocardium shortens. Which subclass of Class I antiarrhythmics most likely produces this effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Class IB, which mildly slows phase 0 and shortens repolarization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Class IA, which moderately slows phase 0 and prolongs repolarization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Class IC, which markedly slows phase 0 with minimal effect on repolarization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Class IA, which shortens the action potential duration and the refractory period

choices.label: E
choices.text: Class IC, which prolongs repolarization more than any other subclass

explanation.summary: Class IB agents (lidocaine, mexiletine, phenytoin) shorten repolarization and the effective refractory period, particularly in ventricular tissue. They have mild effects on phase 0 depolarization and rapid binding/unbinding kinetics.
explanation.whyCorrect: Class IB agents shorten the action potential duration by facilitating potassium efflux during repolarization, which results in a shortened effective refractory period. This is a distinguishing feature from Class IA (which prolongs ERP) and Class IC (minimal effect on ERP).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Class IA agents prolong repolarization and the effective refractory period, not shorten it. This is the opposite of the observed effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Class IC agents markedly slow phase 0 but have minimal effect on repolarization and the refractory period. They do not shorten the ERP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This incorrectly attributes ERP shortening to Class IA agents. Class IA agents prolong both the action potential duration and the refractory period.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Class IC agents do not significantly prolong repolarization. Class IA and Class III agents are the subclasses that prolong repolarization.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with epilepsy and a history of digoxin toxicity-induced ventricular arrhythmias is being treated with a drug that serves dual purposes: controlling his seizures and suppressing his cardiac arrhythmia. Which of the following drugs is most likely being used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carbamazepine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Valproic acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mexiletine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phenytoin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Procainamide

explanation.summary: Phenytoin is a Class IB antiarrhythmic that is also widely used as an antiepileptic drug. It is particularly effective for digoxin toxicity-induced arrhythmias because it suppresses delayed afterdepolarizations (the mechanism of digoxin-induced arrhythmias) without worsening AV block.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phenytoin uniquely serves as both an antiepileptic and an antiarrhythmic. It is specifically useful in digoxin toxicity because it suppresses ventricular arrhythmias while potentially improving AV conduction that digoxin impairs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Carbamazepine is an antiepileptic with sodium channel blocking properties but is not used as an antiarrhythmic agent and can itself cause cardiac conduction abnormalities.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Valproic acid is an antiepileptic that acts through GABA potentiation and sodium channel modulation but has no established antiarrhythmic role.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mexiletine is a Class IB antiarrhythmic (oral lidocaine analogue) but is not used as an antiepileptic drug, so it would not serve the dual purpose described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Procainamide is a Class IA antiarrhythmic with no antiepileptic properties. It is also not preferred in digoxin toxicity due to its effects on conduction.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with persistent atrial fibrillation, COPD, and an ejection fraction of 30% is being considered for rhythm control. His physician chooses amiodarone over sotalol. Which of the following best justifies this decision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amiodarone has no beta-blocking activity, making it safer in COPD

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amiodarone does not prolong the QT interval, reducing torsades risk

choices.label: C
choices.text: Amiodarone is safer than sotalol in reduced ejection fraction due to lower proarrhythmic risk

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amiodarone has a shorter half-life, allowing for easier dose titration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Amiodarone selectively blocks atrial sodium channels without ventricular effects

explanation.summary: Amiodarone is one of the few antiarrhythmics that can be safely used in patients with heart failure and reduced ejection fraction. Sotalol, while effective, carries a higher risk of proarrhythmia (torsades de pointes) especially in patients with heart failure, electrolyte abnormalities, or renal impairment. Amiodarone has a remarkably low incidence of torsades despite prolonging the QT interval.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amiodarone has minimal proarrhythmic risk compared to sotalol, even in patients with structural heart disease and low EF. Sotalol's reverse use-dependence and pure Class III effects increase torsades risk, especially in heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amiodarone does have beta-blocking activity (Class II effect) as part of its multi-channel blocking profile. However, its beta-blockade is noncompetitive and generally well-tolerated.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amiodarone does prolong the QT interval significantly. However, it rarely causes torsades de pointes, which is attributed to its homogeneous prolongation of repolarization.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amiodarone has an extremely long half-life (40-55 days), making dose titration difficult, not easy. This is actually a disadvantage of the drug.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amiodarone blocks sodium, potassium, and calcium channels in both atrial and ventricular tissue. It is not atrial-selective.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man has been on amiodarone for 3 years for atrial fibrillation management. He presents with fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and constipation. His TSH is 18 mU/L (normal 0.5-4.5). Which of the following mechanisms best explains amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amiodarone directly destroys thyroid follicular cells through oxidative stress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amiodarone blocks TSH receptors on thyroid follicular cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Amiodarone inhibits peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 without affecting thyroid gland function

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amiodarone's high iodine content causes a sustained Wolff-Chaikoff effect in susceptible individuals

choices.label: E
choices.text: Amiodarone induces autoimmune thyroiditis through molecular mimicry with thyroglobulin

explanation.summary: Amiodarone contains approximately 37% iodine by weight, and chronic use delivers a massive iodine load. In patients with underlying thyroid susceptibility (especially those with Hashimoto thyroiditis), this excess iodine inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis through a persistent Wolff-Chaikoff effect, leading to hypothyroidism.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Wolff-Chaikoff effect is an autoregulatory mechanism where excess iodine inhibits thyroid peroxidase and thyroid hormone synthesis. In susceptible individuals, the thyroid gland fails to escape this inhibition, resulting in persistent hypothyroidism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Direct thyroid cell destruction is more characteristic of amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis type 2, not hypothyroidism. Hypothyroidism is caused by inhibition of hormone synthesis, not cell destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amiodarone does not block TSH receptors. TSH levels are actually elevated in amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism because the pituitary responds to low thyroid hormone levels.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While amiodarone does inhibit peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion, this alone does not explain clinical hypothyroidism with an elevated TSH, which indicates impaired thyroid gland function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism is not primarily caused by molecular mimicry. While pre-existing autoimmune thyroiditis is a risk factor, the mechanism is iodine excess, not immune-mediated molecular mimicry.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT) due to AV nodal reentry. Adenosine is administered and successfully terminates the arrhythmia. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which adenosine terminates AVNRT?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activation of adenosine A1 receptors increases potassium conductance, hyperpolarizing AV nodal cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blockade of L-type calcium channels reduces calcium influx into AV nodal cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibition of sodium channels in the fast pathway of the AV node

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct stimulation of vagal efferents to the AV node

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blockade of beta-1 adrenergic receptors on AV nodal cells

explanation.summary: Adenosine acts on A1 receptors in the AV node, activating a G-protein coupled inward-rectifier potassium channel (GIRK/IKACh). This increases potassium efflux, hyperpolarizing AV nodal cells and transiently blocking conduction through the AV node, thereby interrupting the reentrant circuit in AVNRT.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adenosine A1 receptor activation opens GIRK channels (IKACh), causing potassium efflux and hyperpolarization of AV nodal cells. This slows conduction and increases the refractory period of the AV node, breaking the reentrant circuit.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Adenosine does inhibit calcium influx indirectly (by reducing cAMP via Gi protein), but the primary and most rapid mechanism is increased potassium conductance through GIRK channels, not direct L-type calcium channel blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.C: AV nodal cells are slow-response cells that depend on calcium channels, not sodium channels, for depolarization. Sodium channel blockade would not effectively terminate AVNRT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adenosine acts directly on A1 receptors in the AV node; it does not stimulate vagal efferents. Its effect mimics vagal action but through a direct purinergic mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenosine does not block beta-1 receptors. It acts through its own purinergic A1 receptors coupled to inhibitory G proteins.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chronic stable angina and paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia is started on verapamil. He also takes simvastatin 40 mg daily. One month later, he develops diffuse myalgias and his creatine kinase is markedly elevated. Which of the following best explains this drug interaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verapamil displaces simvastatin from plasma protein binding sites

choices.label: B
choices.text: Verapamil directly potentiates simvastatin's inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase in muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Verapamil increases renal clearance of simvastatin metabolites, producing toxic intermediates

choices.label: D
choices.text: Verapamil inhibits P-glycoprotein in the intestine, reducing simvastatin absorption

choices.label: E
choices.text: Verapamil inhibits CYP3A4, reducing simvastatin metabolism and increasing its serum levels

explanation.summary: Verapamil is a moderate inhibitor of CYP3A4, the primary enzyme responsible for simvastatin metabolism. Inhibition of CYP3A4 leads to elevated simvastatin plasma levels, increasing the risk of statin-induced myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Simvastatin is extensively metabolized by CYP3A4. Verapamil inhibits this enzyme, leading to decreased hepatic clearance and increased systemic exposure to simvastatin, which dose-dependently increases the risk of myopathy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Protein binding displacement is not the clinically significant mechanism of this interaction. The relevant interaction is enzymatic inhibition of CYP3A4.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Verapamil does not directly potentiate HMG-CoA reductase inhibition. The interaction is pharmacokinetic (increased drug levels), not pharmacodynamic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Verapamil does not increase renal clearance of simvastatin metabolites. The interaction involves decreased hepatic metabolism, not altered renal handling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibition of P-glycoprotein would increase (not reduce) absorption. While verapamil does inhibit P-glycoprotein, the primary mechanism of this clinically significant interaction is CYP3A4 inhibition.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with heart failure (EF 25%) and atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response is in the ICU. The team wants to use a short-acting beta-blocker for rate control that can be quickly titrated. Which of the following is the most appropriate choice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Propranolol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atenolol

choices.label: C
choices.text: Esmolol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nadolol

choices.label: E
choices.text: Carvedilol

explanation.summary: Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting, cardioselective beta-1 blocker administered intravenously. It is rapidly metabolized by red blood cell esterases with a half-life of approximately 9 minutes, allowing for precise titration and rapid offset of effect, which is ideal for critically ill patients where hemodynamic instability is a concern.
explanation.whyCorrect: Esmolol's ultra-short half-life (~9 minutes) and IV administration make it ideal for titration in the ICU. If the patient develops hypotension or bradycardia, the drug effect wears off within minutes of discontinuation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Propranolol is a nonselective beta-blocker with a longer half-life (3-6 hours). It is available IV but cannot be titrated as rapidly as esmolol and may cause more hemodynamic compromise.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Atenolol is a cardioselective beta-blocker but is primarily given orally with a long half-life (6-7 hours), making it unsuitable for rapid IV titration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nadolol is a nonselective beta-blocker with the longest half-life among beta-blockers (14-24 hours) and is only available orally, making it inappropriate for acute rate control.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carvedilol is a nonselective beta-blocker with alpha-1 blocking activity. It is given orally with a moderate half-life and is not suitable for acute IV titration.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man has been taking amiodarone 200 mg daily for 2 years. He presents with progressive dyspnea and a dry cough. A chest CT reveals bilateral pulmonary infiltrates with a ground-glass pattern. Pulmonary function tests show a restrictive pattern with decreased DLCO. Which of the following best describes the pathogenesis of this complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Type I hypersensitivity with eosinophilic infiltration of the alveoli

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct cytotoxic injury and phospholipid accumulation in pneumocytes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Granulomatous inflammation similar to sarcoidosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema due to amiodarone-induced left ventricular dysfunction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Infectious pneumonia from amiodarone-induced immunosuppression

explanation.summary: Amiodarone pulmonary toxicity is one of its most serious adverse effects, occurring in up to 5-7% of patients. The mechanism involves direct cytotoxic injury to type II pneumocytes and accumulation of phospholipids (phospholipidosis) within alveolar cells. Amiodarone and its metabolite desethylamiodarone inhibit lysosomal phospholipases, leading to intracellular phospholipid accumulation and cellular damage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amiodarone accumulates in pulmonary tissue and inhibits phospholipases, causing phospholipid accumulation (phospholipidosis) in pneumocytes. This leads to direct cellular toxicity, inflammation, and progressive pulmonary fibrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amiodarone pulmonary toxicity is not an IgE-mediated or eosinophilic process. It is a direct toxic and inflammatory injury to lung parenchyma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While amiodarone toxicity can sometimes show foamy macrophages on biopsy, it is not a granulomatous process like sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amiodarone has minimal negative inotropic effect at therapeutic doses and does not typically cause left ventricular dysfunction or cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amiodarone is not an immunosuppressant and does not predispose to infectious pneumonia. The pulmonary infiltrates are from direct drug toxicity.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with a history of Prinzmetal angina develops symptomatic bradycardia. Her current medications include diltiazem for her angina. Which of the following antiarrhythmic properties of diltiazem most directly contributes to this adverse effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blockade of fast sodium channels in the SA node

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enhancement of vagal tone through central nervous system mechanisms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blockade of delayed rectifier potassium channels prolonging nodal refractory periods

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of L-type calcium channels in the SA and AV nodes, slowing phase 4 depolarization and conduction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibition of If (funny current) channels in sinoatrial pacemaker cells

explanation.summary: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker (Class IV antiarrhythmic) that blocks L-type calcium channels. The SA node and AV node depend on calcium channels for phase 4 depolarization and impulse conduction. Blockade of these channels slows the rate of phase 4 depolarization in SA node pacemaker cells (causing bradycardia) and slows AV nodal conduction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The SA and AV nodes are slow-response tissues that depend on L-type calcium channels for pacemaker activity and conduction. Diltiazem blocks these channels, directly slowing heart rate and AV conduction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SA node pacemaker cells do not depend on fast sodium channels for depolarization. They use calcium channels (L-type and T-type) for the upstroke of the action potential.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Diltiazem does not enhance vagal tone through central mechanisms. Its bradycardic effect is from direct calcium channel blockade in nodal tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diltiazem primarily blocks calcium channels, not delayed rectifier potassium channels. Potassium channel blockade is the mechanism of Class III agents.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The If (funny current) is blocked by ivabradine, not diltiazem. Diltiazem's effect on heart rate is mediated through L-type calcium channel blockade.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man presents with new-onset atrial fibrillation and a ventricular rate of 150 bpm. He is hemodynamically stable. His medical history includes severe asthma requiring daily inhaled corticosteroids and occasional oral prednisone. Which of the following is the most appropriate agent for acute rate control?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Metoprolol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Propranolol

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sotalol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diltiazem

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atenolol

explanation.summary: In patients with severe asthma, beta-blockers (even cardioselective ones) carry a risk of bronchospasm and should be used with extreme caution or avoided. Diltiazem, a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker, provides effective AV nodal blockade for rate control without the risk of bronchoconstriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Diltiazem slows AV nodal conduction via L-type calcium channel blockade and does not affect bronchial smooth muscle beta-2 receptors. It is the preferred rate control agent in patients with severe reactive airway disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metoprolol is cardioselective (beta-1 selective) but selectivity is incomplete, especially at higher doses needed for rate control. It can still provoke bronchospasm in severe asthma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Propranolol is a nonselective beta-blocker that blocks both beta-1 and beta-2 receptors. Beta-2 blockade in the lungs would cause significant bronchoconstriction and is strongly contraindicated in asthma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sotalol is a nonselective beta-blocker with additional Class III properties. It is contraindicated in asthma due to beta-2 blockade and is also not appropriate for acute rate control alone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atenolol is cardioselective but, like metoprolol, selectivity is dose-dependent and may be lost at higher doses, posing a risk in severe asthma.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man on chronic amiodarone therapy presents with a painless neck mass. Thyroid function tests reveal a suppressed TSH, elevated free T4, and elevated free T3. Radioactive iodine uptake is low. Color Doppler ultrasonography shows decreased vascularity of the thyroid gland. Which type of amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis does this patient most likely have?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Type 1 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis due to iodine-induced excess thyroid hormone synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Type 2 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis due to destructive thyroiditis with preformed hormone release

choices.label: C
choices.text: Subacute granulomatous thyroiditis triggered by amiodarone-induced viral reactivation

choices.label: D
choices.text: TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma stimulated by amiodarone

choices.label: E
choices.text: Graves disease unmasked by amiodarone's iodine load

explanation.summary: Type 2 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis (AIT) is a destructive thyroiditis caused by direct amiodarone toxicity to thyroid follicular cells. It is characterized by low radioactive iodine uptake (because the gland is not actively synthesizing hormone; preformed hormone is being released from damaged cells) and decreased vascularity on Doppler. This contrasts with Type 1 AIT, which involves excess hormone synthesis and typically shows increased vascularity.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of low RAIU, decreased thyroid vascularity, and thyrotoxicosis points to a destructive process (Type 2 AIT) where preformed thyroid hormone leaks from damaged follicular cells, not active hormone synthesis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Type 1 AIT involves excess hormone synthesis driven by iodine excess, typically in patients with underlying thyroid disease. It would show increased or normal vascularity on Doppler, not decreased.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Subacute granulomatous (de Quervain) thyroiditis is caused by viral infections and presents with thyroid pain and tenderness. Amiodarone does not cause viral reactivation, and the mass was painless.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A TSH-secreting adenoma would present with elevated (not suppressed) TSH. The suppressed TSH in this patient excludes this diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Graves disease would show increased RAIU and increased vascularity on Doppler, not the decreased values seen in this patient.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with atrial fibrillation is started on sotalol. Three days later, he collapses and an ECG shows polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with a prolonged QTc of 580 ms. Which of the following properties of sotalol is most directly responsible for this arrhythmia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Beta-1 receptor blockade causing excessive bradycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sodium channel blockade slowing conduction and creating reentrant circuits

choices.label: C
choices.text: Calcium channel blockade causing early afterdepolarizations in Purkinje fibers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alpha-1 receptor blockade causing hypotension and reflex sympathetic activation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blockade of the delayed rectifier potassium current (IKr), prolonging repolarization and creating dispersion of refractoriness

explanation.summary: Sotalol has both Class II (beta-blocker) and Class III (potassium channel blocker) properties. Its Class III effect involves blockade of the rapid delayed rectifier potassium current (IKr), which prolongs the action potential duration and QT interval. This prolongation can create dispersion of refractoriness and trigger early afterdepolarizations, leading to torsades de pointes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sotalol's IKr blockade prolongs ventricular repolarization, creating heterogeneous recovery of excitability. This dispersion of refractoriness provides the substrate for early afterdepolarizations and triggered activity, manifesting as torsades de pointes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While sotalol's beta-blockade can cause bradycardia, which may contribute to QT prolongation, the direct mechanism of torsades is IKr blockade causing repolarization heterogeneity, not bradycardia alone.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sotalol does not have significant sodium channel blocking activity. It is not a Class I antiarrhythmic, and sodium channel blockade would cause QRS widening rather than QT prolongation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sotalol does not block calcium channels. Early afterdepolarizations in the setting of sotalol are caused by reactivation of calcium and sodium channels during prolonged repolarization, not by calcium channel blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sotalol does not have alpha-1 blocking activity. It is a nonselective beta-blocker without alpha-receptor effects.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with Raynaud phenomenon and paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia requires long-term rate control therapy. Her cardiologist wants to use a beta-blocker but is concerned about peripheral vasoconstriction. Which of the following beta-blockers has additional alpha-1 blocking activity that would make it the most appropriate choice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Metoprolol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atenolol

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carvedilol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bisoprolol

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nadolol

explanation.summary: Carvedilol is a nonselective beta-blocker with additional alpha-1 receptor blocking activity. The alpha-1 blockade causes vasodilation, which can counteract the peripheral vasoconstriction caused by unopposed alpha-receptor stimulation when beta-2 receptors are blocked. This makes it a better choice in patients with peripheral vascular disease or Raynaud phenomenon compared to pure beta-blockers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carvedilol's alpha-1 blockade produces peripheral vasodilation, offsetting the vasoconstriction that occurs with beta-blocker use. This property makes it more suitable for patients with Raynaud phenomenon.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metoprolol is a cardioselective beta-1 blocker without alpha-blocking activity. It may still worsen Raynaud symptoms due to reduced cardiac output and unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Atenolol is a cardioselective beta-1 blocker without alpha-blocking properties. It offers no peripheral vasodilatory benefit.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bisoprolol is highly cardioselective for beta-1 receptors but lacks alpha-blocking activity and would not provide peripheral vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nadolol is a nonselective beta-blocker without alpha-blocking activity. Nonselective beta-blockade (including beta-2) may actually worsen peripheral vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man is taking verapamil for rate control of atrial fibrillation. He is admitted with acute decompensated heart failure. His physician attributes the heart failure in part to verapamil therapy. Which of the following mechanisms best explains how verapamil can precipitate or worsen heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verapamil increases afterload through peripheral vasoconstriction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Verapamil blocks L-type calcium channels in cardiac myocytes, reducing contractility

choices.label: C
choices.text: Verapamil activates the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, causing volume overload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Verapamil inhibits sodium-potassium ATPase, causing myocyte calcium overload and diastolic dysfunction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Verapamil causes direct myocardial fibrosis through collagen deposition

explanation.summary: Verapamil blocks L-type calcium channels in cardiac myocytes, reducing calcium entry during phase 2 of the action potential. This directly decreases excitation-contraction coupling and myocardial contractility (negative inotropy), which can precipitate or worsen heart failure, particularly in patients with pre-existing ventricular dysfunction.
explanation.whyCorrect: By blocking L-type calcium channels in the myocardium, verapamil reduces the calcium available for actin-myosin cross-bridge cycling, directly decreasing contractile force and potentially decompensating heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Verapamil causes peripheral vasodilation (reduced afterload) through arterial smooth muscle calcium channel blockade, not vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Verapamil does not directly activate the RAAS. In fact, calcium channel blockers may have neutral or inhibitory effects on the RAAS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibition of sodium-potassium ATPase is the mechanism of digoxin, not verapamil. Verapamil acts on L-type calcium channels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Verapamil does not cause myocardial fibrosis. Its negative inotropic effect is a functional impairment of contractility, not a structural change.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man undergoes percutaneous coronary intervention with drug-eluting stent placement for an acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction. He is started on dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and ticagrelor. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of ticagrelor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Irreversible inhibition of cyclooxygenase-1 in platelets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Irreversible binding to the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reversible allosteric antagonism of the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of phosphodiesterase III, increasing intraplatelet cAMP

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blockade of glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptors on the platelet surface

explanation.summary: Ticagrelor is a cyclopentyl-triazolopyrimidine that directly and reversibly binds to the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets at an allosteric site, distinct from the ADP binding site. Unlike clopidogrel and prasugrel, which are irreversible P2Y12 inhibitors requiring hepatic activation, ticagrelor is an active drug that provides reversible, direct-acting inhibition.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ticagrelor binds reversibly to the P2Y12 receptor at a site distinct from ADP binding (allosteric), providing consistent and predictable antiplatelet effects without requiring metabolic activation. Its reversibility means platelet function recovers more quickly after discontinuation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Irreversible COX-1 inhibition is the mechanism of aspirin, not ticagrelor. Aspirin acetylates COX-1, blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Irreversible P2Y12 binding describes clopidogrel and prasugrel, which form permanent disulfide bonds with the receptor. Ticagrelor's binding is reversible.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phosphodiesterase III inhibition is the mechanism of cilostazol and dipyridamole, which increase cAMP levels in platelets. This is not how ticagrelor works.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor blockade is the mechanism of abciximab, eptifibatide, and tirofiban. These agents block the final common pathway of platelet aggregation.

questions.stem: A 57-year-old man with a recent coronary stent is on clopidogrel 75 mg daily. Despite adherence, he suffers a subacute stent thrombosis. Genetic testing reveals he is a CYP2C19 poor metabolizer. Which of the following best explains why clopidogrel failed in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clopidogrel is directly inactivated by CYP2C19, and poor metabolizers accumulate toxic levels of the parent drug

choices.label: B
choices.text: CYP2C19 poor metabolizers have increased platelet P2Y12 receptor expression, overwhelming the drug's effect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Poor metabolizers have enhanced hepatic clearance of clopidogrel's active metabolite

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clopidogrel is a prodrug requiring CYP2C19-mediated bioactivation, and poor metabolizers generate insufficient active metabolite

choices.label: E
choices.text: CYP2C19 polymorphisms alter platelet membrane composition, preventing drug-receptor binding

explanation.summary: Clopidogrel is a prodrug that requires two-step hepatic oxidation to generate its active thiol metabolite. CYP2C19 is the key enzyme in this bioactivation pathway. Patients who are CYP2C19 poor metabolizers (*2/*2 or *2/*3 genotypes) produce significantly less active metabolite, resulting in inadequate platelet inhibition and increased risk of thrombotic events, including stent thrombosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because CYP2C19 is essential for converting clopidogrel to its active metabolite, poor metabolizers cannot generate sufficient active drug to adequately inhibit P2Y12 receptors, leading to clopidogrel resistance and therapeutic failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CYP2C19 activates clopidogrel, not inactivates it. Poor metabolizers have less activation, not toxic accumulation of the parent drug.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CYP2C19 polymorphisms affect hepatic drug metabolism, not platelet receptor expression. Receptor density is not altered by this genetic variant.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Poor metabolizers produce less active metabolite due to impaired formation, not enhanced clearance. The issue is insufficient generation of the active drug.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CYP2C19 is a hepatic enzyme and does not affect platelet membrane composition or drug-receptor binding characteristics.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with a history of transient ischemic attack is started on aspirin 81 mg daily for secondary stroke prevention. Which of the following best describes how aspirin prevents platelet aggregation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Irreversible acetylation of cyclooxygenase-1 in platelets, blocking thromboxane A2 production

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reversible inhibition of cyclooxygenase-2 in platelets, reducing prostaglandin E2 synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Competitive antagonism of ADP at the P2Y12 receptor on the platelet surface

choices.label: D
choices.text: Activation of prostacyclin receptors on platelets, increasing intracellular cAMP

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibition of thrombin-mediated activation of protease-activated receptor-1 on platelets

explanation.summary: Aspirin irreversibly acetylates a serine residue (Ser-530) in the active site of cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) in platelets. This prevents the conversion of arachidonic acid to thromboxane A2, a potent platelet activator and vasoconstrictor. Because platelets are anucleate and cannot synthesize new COX-1, the inhibition lasts for the platelet's entire lifespan (~7-10 days).
explanation.whyCorrect: Aspirin's irreversible acetylation of COX-1 eliminates thromboxane A2 production for the life of the platelet. TXA2 normally promotes platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction, so its absence reduces platelet activation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspirin at low doses preferentially inhibits COX-1, not COX-2. The inhibition is irreversible (covalent acetylation), not reversible. PGE2 is not the primary prostanoid involved in platelet aggregation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ADP receptor antagonism at P2Y12 describes the thienopyridines (clopidogrel, prasugrel) and ticagrelor, not aspirin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspirin does not activate prostacyclin receptors. Prostacyclin (PGI2) is produced by endothelial cells and inhibits platelets, but aspirin actually reduces prostacyclin synthesis at high doses.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Protease-activated receptor-1 (PAR-1) inhibition describes vorapaxar, not aspirin.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with peripheral arterial disease and intermittent claudication is started on cilostazol. In addition to its antiplatelet effects, which of the following additional therapeutic benefits does cilostazol provide for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct thrombolysis of existing arterial thrombi

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduction of LDL cholesterol through HMG-CoA reductase inhibition

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anticoagulant activity through factor Xa inhibition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduction of blood viscosity through red blood cell deformability changes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vasodilation of peripheral arteries and increased walking distance

explanation.summary: Cilostazol is a phosphodiesterase III (PDE3) inhibitor that increases intracellular cAMP in both platelets and vascular smooth muscle cells. In platelets, increased cAMP inhibits aggregation. In vascular smooth muscle, increased cAMP causes vasodilation. This dual action makes cilostazol particularly useful in intermittent claudication, where it improves walking distance by 40-60% compared to placebo.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cilostazol's PDE3 inhibition raises cAMP in vascular smooth muscle, producing vasodilation. This, combined with its antiplatelet effect, improves blood flow to ischemic limbs and increases pain-free walking distance in claudication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cilostazol is an antiplatelet and vasodilator, not a thrombolytic agent. It does not dissolve existing clots; thrombolytics such as alteplase do that.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cilostazol does not inhibit HMG-CoA reductase. Statins are the drug class that lowers LDL cholesterol through this mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cilostazol does not inhibit factor Xa. Anticoagulants such as rivaroxaban and apixaban act through this mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While pentoxifylline is thought to improve red blood cell deformability, cilostazol's primary mechanism involves PDE3 inhibition leading to vasodilation and antiplatelet effects, not rheologic changes.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man is in the cardiac catheterization laboratory undergoing primary PCI for an acute STEMI. The interventional cardiologist administers abciximab intravenously. Thirty minutes later, the patient develops severe thrombocytopenia with a platelet count of 20,000/µL. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of abciximab-induced thrombocytopenia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct toxic suppression of megakaryocyte production in the bone marrow

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immune-mediated destruction through antibodies against drug-induced neoepitopes on GP IIb/IIIa

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sequestration of platelets in the spleen due to complement activation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Platelet consumption through widespread microvascular thrombosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Heparin-dependent antibody formation triggered by co-administration of unfractionated heparin

explanation.summary: Abciximab-induced thrombocytopenia can occur rapidly (within hours) due to naturally occurring antibodies that recognize neoepitopes formed when abciximab binds to GP IIb/IIIa receptors. These antibodies bind to the drug-receptor complex, leading to opsonization and rapid clearance of platelets by the reticuloendothelial system. This is distinct from heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Abciximab (a chimeric monoclonal antibody Fab fragment) binds GP IIb/IIIa, creating new epitopes. Pre-existing or rapidly formed antibodies recognize these neoepitopes, causing immune-mediated platelet destruction, sometimes within minutes to hours of first exposure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The rapid onset (within 30 minutes) excludes bone marrow suppression, which takes days to weeks to manifest as thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Complement-mediated splenic sequestration is not the established mechanism of GP IIb/IIIa inhibitor-induced thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet consumption through microvascular thrombosis describes thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura or disseminated intravascular coagulation, not GP IIb/IIIa inhibitor-induced thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is a separate entity caused by antibodies against heparin-PF4 complexes, typically occurring 5-10 days after heparin exposure, not within 30 minutes.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease is taking clopidogrel. He develops a peptic ulcer and is started on omeprazole. His cardiologist expresses concern about a potential drug interaction. Which of the following best explains the basis of this concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Omeprazole increases gastric pH, reducing clopidogrel absorption

choices.label: B
choices.text: Omeprazole competes with clopidogrel for binding at the P2Y12 receptor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Omeprazole induces hepatic CYP3A4, accelerating clopidogrel clearance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, reducing conversion of clopidogrel to its active metabolite

choices.label: E
choices.text: Omeprazole directly inactivates the active metabolite of clopidogrel in the bloodstream

explanation.summary: Omeprazole is a competitive inhibitor of CYP2C19, the same enzyme required to convert clopidogrel (a prodrug) to its active thiol metabolite. Co-administration may reduce the formation of the active metabolite, potentially diminishing clopidogrel's antiplatelet efficacy. The FDA issued a warning about this interaction, though clinical significance remains debated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19-mediated bioactivation of clopidogrel, reducing active metabolite generation and potentially attenuating platelet inhibition. This pharmacokinetic interaction prompted FDA advisories recommending alternative PPIs like pantoprazole.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Clopidogrel absorption is not significantly pH-dependent. The interaction is at the level of hepatic metabolism, not gastrointestinal absorption.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Omeprazole has no activity at the P2Y12 receptor. It is a proton pump inhibitor that acts on H+/K+ ATPase in gastric parietal cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Omeprazole is a CYP2C19 inhibitor, not a CYP3A4 inducer. It decreases clopidogrel activation rather than accelerating its clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Omeprazole does not directly interact with clopidogrel's active metabolite in the bloodstream. The interaction occurs in the liver at the level of enzymatic activation.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old woman with a history of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and a prior myocardial infarction is being considered for vorapaxar. She also has a history of a lacunar stroke 2 years ago. Which of the following is the primary reason vorapaxar is contraindicated in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vorapaxar causes severe thrombocytopenia in patients with prior cerebrovascular events

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vorapaxar increases the risk of intracranial hemorrhage in patients with a history of stroke or TIA

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vorapaxar is ineffective in patients with lacunar infarcts due to small vessel pathology

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vorapaxar interacts with tissue plasminogen activator previously used for stroke treatment

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vorapaxar causes paradoxical thrombosis in patients with cerebrovascular disease

explanation.summary: Vorapaxar, a protease-activated receptor-1 (PAR-1) antagonist, is contraindicated in patients with a history of stroke or TIA because clinical trials (TRA 2P-TIMI 50) showed a significantly increased risk of intracranial hemorrhage in this subgroup. The FDA labeling carries a black box warning against use in patients with prior stroke or TIA.
explanation.whyCorrect: The TRA 2P-TIMI 50 trial demonstrated that vorapaxar significantly increased intracranial hemorrhage in patients with a history of stroke or TIA, leading to a contraindication and black box warning for this population.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Vorapaxar does not cause significant thrombocytopenia. Its primary safety concern in stroke patients is intracranial hemorrhage, not platelet count reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The contraindication applies to all types of prior stroke, not just lacunar infarcts. It is not related to efficacy differences by stroke subtype but to hemorrhagic risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no specific interaction between vorapaxar and tPA. The contraindication is based on the inherent bleeding risk in patients with cerebrovascular disease history.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vorapaxar does not cause paradoxical thrombosis. As a PAR-1 antagonist, it inhibits thrombin-mediated platelet activation; the concern is bleeding, not thrombosis.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man on ticagrelor for a recent acute coronary syndrome develops dyspnea without evidence of heart failure or pulmonary disease. His oxygen saturation is 97% on room air and his chest X-ray is normal. Which of the following best explains this side effect of ticagrelor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ticagrelor inhibits adenosine reuptake, increasing local adenosine concentrations that stimulate pulmonary C-fiber receptors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ticagrelor causes pulmonary fibrosis through direct toxicity to type II pneumocytes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ticagrelor induces bronchospasm through leukotriene overproduction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ticagrelor causes pulmonary hypertension through thromboxane receptor agonism

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ticagrelor produces methemoglobinemia, reducing oxygen-carrying capacity

explanation.summary: Ticagrelor-associated dyspnea is a well-recognized side effect occurring in 10-15% of patients. Ticagrelor inhibits equilibrative nucleoside transporter-1 (ENT1), which is responsible for adenosine reuptake into cells. This increases local adenosine concentrations, which can stimulate pulmonary vagal C-fiber receptors, producing the sensation of dyspnea without actual pulmonary pathology.
explanation.whyCorrect: By inhibiting ENT1, ticagrelor increases extracellular adenosine levels. Adenosine activates pulmonary C-fiber afferents and central chemoreceptors, causing the subjective sensation of dyspnea. This is generally self-limiting and does not indicate cardiopulmonary compromise.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Ticagrelor does not cause pulmonary fibrosis. This is a concern with amiodarone, not antiplatelet agents.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ticagrelor does not promote leukotriene synthesis or cause bronchospasm. Aspirin-sensitive asthma involves leukotriene overproduction, but this is specific to COX inhibition, not P2Y12 antagonism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ticagrelor does not act on thromboxane receptors and does not cause pulmonary hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ticagrelor does not cause methemoglobinemia. Dapsone and nitrites are drugs associated with this condition.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with an acute STEMI undergoes PCI with stent placement. He is loaded with prasugrel 60 mg in addition to aspirin. Compared to clopidogrel, which of the following best describes a pharmacologic advantage of prasugrel?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prasugrel provides reversible P2Y12 inhibition, allowing faster platelet recovery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prasugrel does not require hepatic metabolism for activation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prasugrel has more consistent and rapid active metabolite generation with less interpatient variability

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prasugrel acts on both P2Y1 and P2Y12 receptors simultaneously

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prasugrel has a shorter half-life, allowing for easier perioperative management

explanation.summary: Prasugrel, like clopidogrel, is a thienopyridine prodrug requiring hepatic activation. However, prasugrel requires only one CYP-dependent oxidation step (versus two for clopidogrel) and its intestinal hydrolysis generates an intermediate that is more efficiently converted to the active metabolite. This results in faster onset, higher active metabolite levels, and significantly less interpatient variability compared to clopidogrel, with less susceptibility to CYP2C19 polymorphisms.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prasugrel's single-step CYP activation and more efficient metabolic conversion produce more consistent active metabolite levels with less variability due to genetic polymorphisms, resulting in more reliable and potent platelet inhibition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Prasugrel is an irreversible P2Y12 inhibitor (like clopidogrel), not reversible. Ticagrelor is the reversible P2Y12 antagonist.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Prasugrel does require hepatic CYP metabolism for activation, but it requires fewer metabolic steps than clopidogrel, making it more efficient, not metabolism-independent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Prasugrel is selective for the P2Y12 receptor. It does not act on P2Y1 receptors. No currently used antiplatelet agent targets both P2Y subtypes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prasugrel has a longer duration of effect than clopidogrel on platelets, and its irreversible binding means platelet function takes days to recover, making perioperative management more challenging, not easier.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old woman weighing 55 kg with a history of TIA and a recent NSTEMI is being considered for dual antiplatelet therapy. The cardiologist decides against prasugrel. Which of the following patient characteristics most strongly supports this decision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Her age greater than 75 years and body weight less than 60 kg increase the risk of major bleeding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prasugrel is contraindicated in all female patients due to hormone-drug interactions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prasugrel requires CYP2D6 metabolism, which declines significantly with age

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prasugrel causes excessive QT prolongation in elderly patients

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prasugrel has reduced efficacy in patients with NSTEMI compared to STEMI

explanation.summary: Prasugrel carries an FDA black box warning for patients aged 75 years or older and those weighing less than 60 kg, as these populations have an increased risk of major bleeding, including fatal bleeding, that may outweigh the antiplatelet benefit. Additionally, prasugrel is contraindicated in patients with prior stroke or TIA. This patient has multiple risk factors that argue against prasugrel use.
explanation.whyCorrect: The TRITON-TIMI 38 trial showed that patients aged 75+ and those weighing <60 kg had increased major bleeding events with prasugrel. Combined with her history of TIA (an absolute contraindication), prasugrel should be avoided.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Prasugrel is not contraindicated in all females. The concern is specific to age, weight, and history of cerebrovascular events, not sex-based interactions.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prasugrel is primarily metabolized by CYP3A4 and CYP2B6, not CYP2D6. Age-related metabolic decline is not the primary pharmacokinetic concern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Prasugrel does not cause QT prolongation. This is not a known adverse effect of thienopyridine antiplatelet agents.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prasugrel is effective in both STEMI and NSTEMI. The TRITON-TIMI 38 trial included ACS patients broadly and showed benefit across the spectrum, with safety concerns in specific subgroups.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with a mechanical mitral valve prosthesis is on warfarin anticoagulation. She asks her physician if she can take aspirin instead, as she finds warfarin inconvenient. Which of the following best explains why aspirin alone is insufficient for her mechanical valve?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin only inhibits platelet adhesion to collagen but does not prevent platelet aggregation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mechanical valves generate high shear stress that activates the coagulation cascade, requiring factor inhibition rather than platelet inhibition alone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aspirin's antiplatelet effect is too short-lived, lasting only 2-4 hours

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mechanical valves only generate venous-type thrombi that are resistant to antiplatelet therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aspirin causes hemolysis when used with mechanical valves

explanation.summary: Mechanical heart valves create non-endothelialized surfaces that activate the contact (intrinsic) coagulation pathway and generate high shear stress, both of which promote fibrin-rich thrombus formation through the coagulation cascade. Antiplatelet agents like aspirin primarily inhibit platelet aggregation but do not adequately suppress the coagulation cascade. Warfarin, by inhibiting vitamin K-dependent clotting factors, is necessary to prevent the fibrin-rich thrombi that form on mechanical valves.
explanation.whyCorrect: Thrombus formation on mechanical valves is driven primarily by contact activation of the coagulation cascade on foreign surfaces under high shear stress. This generates fibrin-rich thrombi that require coagulation factor inhibition (warfarin), not just platelet inhibition (aspirin).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aspirin inhibits thromboxane A2-mediated platelet aggregation, not just adhesion. However, even complete platelet inhibition is insufficient because the dominant thrombotic mechanism involves the coagulation cascade.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1 for the entire lifespan of the platelet (7-10 days). Its antiplatelet effect is long-lasting, not short-lived.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mechanical valve thrombi are arterial-type thrombi forming under high flow conditions, not venous-type thrombi. However, they are fibrin-rich due to contact activation rather than purely platelet-rich.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aspirin does not cause hemolysis with mechanical valves. Mechanical hemolytic anemia can occur from the valve itself but is not caused by aspirin.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man on dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel requires urgent coronary artery bypass grafting. The surgical team is concerned about perioperative bleeding. Which of the following strategies is most appropriate regarding antiplatelet management before surgery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue both agents up to the day of surgery, as stopping them increases thrombotic risk

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discontinue aspirin 3 days before surgery but continue clopidogrel

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discontinue clopidogrel at least 5 days before surgery while continuing aspirin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer platelet transfusion immediately before surgery while continuing both agents

choices.label: E
choices.text: Switch to intravenous tirofiban 24 hours before surgery for bridging antiplatelet therapy

explanation.summary: Current guidelines recommend discontinuing clopidogrel at least 5 days (preferably 5-7 days) before elective CABG to allow sufficient platelet turnover and recovery of platelet function, reducing the risk of major perioperative bleeding. Aspirin is generally continued because it has been shown to reduce graft occlusion and does not significantly increase surgical bleeding risk in CABG.
explanation.whyCorrect: Clopidogrel irreversibly inhibits P2Y12 receptors, and affected platelets cannot aggregate normally for their remaining lifespan. A 5-day washout allows approximately 50% of platelets to be replaced with uninhibited ones, reducing bleeding risk. Aspirin continuation is supported by evidence of improved graft patency.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Continuing clopidogrel up to surgery significantly increases major bleeding, blood transfusion requirements, and the need for surgical re-exploration. Guidelines recommend discontinuation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspirin should generally be continued before CABG, not stopped, as it improves graft patency. It is clopidogrel that should be discontinued due to its greater contribution to surgical bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet transfusion while the drug is still active would result in transfused platelets also being inhibited by circulating drug. The appropriate strategy is to allow drug washout before surgery.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bridging with a GP IIb/IIIa inhibitor like tirofiban adds bleeding risk and is not standard practice before CABG. The preferred approach is simply holding clopidogrel with appropriate timing.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation and a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 4 is started on rivaroxaban for stroke prophylaxis. She has a creatinine clearance of 42 mL/min. Three weeks later, she presents with melena and a hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL. Her last dose of rivaroxaban was 2 hours ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate reversal agent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protamine sulfate intravenously to neutralize the anti-factor Xa activity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Andexanet alfa intravenously to bind and sequester rivaroxaban

choices.label: C
choices.text: Idarucizumab intravenously to directly bind and inactivate rivaroxaban

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fresh frozen plasma transfusion to replenish all depleted clotting factors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vitamin K intravenously to restore hepatic synthesis of clotting factors
